scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 5 anti-patterns identified by name — shared DB, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-based background jobs, direct cross-service DB reads. (2) Remediation is clearly prioritized across 4 phases, not treated as equally urgent. (3) Shared database and direct DB reads both marked CRITICAL and addressed in Phase 2 as highest-risk items (async notifications placed first only due to lower migration risk/higher ROI ratio). (4) REST-based jobs explicitly called out as fragile due to timeout mismatch, lack of idempotency, missing observability, and attack surface. (5) Trade-offs for shared DB fix detailed in both the trade-offs table and risks table — includes migration complexity, dual-write strategy, and the need for query audit. (6) Incremental remediation via strangler fig, dual-write, expand-contract — explicitly warns against big-bang approach.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — each anti-pattern includes not just identification but second-order effects (e.g., shared DB making it psychologically easy to add joins, retry storms from sync fan-in)
Direct DB reads correctly linked back to shared-DB anti-pattern ('conceptually the same shared-database anti-pattern, just unidirectional')
Prioritization rationale is well-reasoned — async notifications first for highest ROI/lowest risk, DB split second after audit prerequisite
Explicit pitfalls-to-avoid section prevents common implementation mistakes (shared event library recreating coupling, skipping query audit)
Trade-offs table includes alternatives considered and why they were rejected, not just gains/sacrifices

Notification fan-in remediation is placed in Phase 1 (highest priority) while the rubric expects it as medium-term — though the agent's reasoning for this ordering is defensible (lowest risk, highest ROI)
Output is very long — could be more concise while still meeting all criteria

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy) and the synchronous Notification Service fan-in (creating a single point of failure across 5 services). Left unaddressed, these patterns will increasingly constrain delivery velocity, cause cascading failures, and make independent deployment imposs
